heroOverview: Mastodynia — clinical breast pain — affects approximately 70% of women at some point during their lives, yet fewer than 15% seek medical attention. The pain arises from a complex interplay of hormonal fluctuations, local breast tissue changes, and referred musculoskeletal signals. Most cases are cyclical, closely tied to the menstrual cycle, and resolve without treatment or with simple measures. Non-cyclical breast pain is less common, tends to affect a localised area, and has a broader differential that includes benign breast disease, medication side effects, and chest-wall disorders. Distinguishing between these patterns guides management and usually allows reassurance that cancer is not the cause — breast cancer presents with pain as an isolated symptom in fewer than 3% of cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 70% of women at some point in their lives; around 11% report severe or disabling pain (Royal College of Surgeons guidelines)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in women aged 30-50 during reproductive years; cyclical breast pain is the predominant subtype
keyStats.avgAge: Peak cyclical breast pain in women aged 30-40; non-cyclical pain more common after 40
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 70 million women experience significant breast pain annually worldwide

definition: Mastodynia (ICD-10: N64.4) is pain originating in or perceived as arising from breast tissue. It is classified as cyclical when it follows the menstrual cycle, typically worsening in the 7-10 days before menstruation and resolving with the onset of menses, and non-cyclical when it is unrelated to the menstrual cycle and is often more persistent, focal, or burning in character. A third category — extramammary pain — refers to musculoskeletal or chest-wall pain that radiates to the breast and is not truly breast tissue in origin. Cyclical mastodynia accounts for roughly two-thirds of cases and reflects the sensitivity of breast epithelium and stroma to fluctuating oestrogen and progesterone levels throughout the menstrual cycle. Non-cyclical pain represents the remaining third and may arise from benign structural changes such as cysts, fibroadenomata, duct ectasia, or infection; from medications including selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors, antipsychotics, hormone therapy, and spironolactone; or from referred pain from the costochondral junctions, intercostal muscles, or cervical or thoracic spine. Mastodynia falls under general surgery or breast surgery for investigation and under primary care for initial assessment and management. It is among the most common breast complaints in clinical practice.

typesClassification.description: Bilateral, diffuse aching or heaviness that worsens in the luteal phase and resolves with menstruation; the most common pattern, accounting for about 65% of breast pain presentations.
Constant or intermittent pain that is unrelated to the menstrual cycle, often unilateral and localised; linked to benign breast disease, medications, or structural causes.
Pain arising from the costal cartilages, intercostal muscles, or the pectoralis major muscle that is felt in the breast area; reproduced by pressing on the chest wall rather than the breast itself.

primarySymptoms: A dull, aching, or throbbing heaviness in one or both breasts, often the first and most characteristic feature of cyclical breast pain.
Tenderness to light touch or clothing contact across the outer quadrants of the breast, which are the areas richest in hormone-sensitive tissue.
Breast swelling or a sense of engorgement that worsens in the week before menstruation and subsides within a day or two of its onset.
Sharp or burning pain in a localised area of the breast, more typical of non-cyclical mastodynia, which may be constant or triggered by movement.
Radiation of pain into the axilla (armpit) or down the inner arm, reflecting the shared nerve supply of the upper outer breast and the brachial plexus.
Nipple sensitivity or tenderness, which occurs in both cyclical and non-cyclical forms and is not a marker of malignancy in isolation.
A palpable lumpiness or nodularity felt bilaterally, corresponding to areas of benign fibrocystic change that become more prominent before menstruation.
Pain that disrupts sleep when turning or lying on the affected side, a common complaint that distinguishes moderate-to-severe mastodynia from mild background discomfort.
Pain reproduced by pressing on the costal cartilage medial to the breast (Tietze sign), suggesting a chest-wall contribution to perceived breast pain.
Unilateral, persistent focal pain at a single point in the breast, particularly in postmenopausal women, which warrants imaging even when examination is normal.
earlyWarningSigns: Breast discomfort that begins predictably three to seven days before the expected period and is relieved by its onset.
A slight increase in breast firmness or fullness that appears each month in the same phase of the cycle.
Increased sensitivity to bra straps or chest-wall pressure that was not previously present.
A vague ache in the armpit that accompanies breast fullness.
emergencySigns: A new, hard, fixed, or irregularly shaped breast lump accompanying pain — this requires urgent assessment even if the pain itself suggests a benign cause.
Skin changes over the painful area: redness, warmth, thickening, peau d'orange texture, or a retracted nipple, which raise the possibility of inflammatory breast cancer or abscess.
Fever, heat, and redness consistent with mastitis or a breast abscess, particularly in breastfeeding or recently postpartum women.
Unilateral spontaneous nipple discharge (especially bloody or clear) alongside new breast pain in a woman over 40.
Persistent, non-cyclical breast pain in a postmenopausal woman not using hormone therapy.

causes.cause: Hormonal fluctuations across the menstrual cycle
causes.description: Rising oestrogen and progesterone in the luteal phase increase breast epithelial proliferation and ductal and stromal water retention, sensitising stretch receptors and nociceptors in breast tissue and producing the characteristic premenstrual aching and heaviness of cyclical mastodynia.

Microcysts, adenosis, and stromal fibrosis alter the mechanical environment of breast tissue; distension of cysts or ductal structures increases local pressure and activates pain fibres, producing focal or diffuse non-cyclical pain that may worsen cyclically in women who are still menstruating.

Several drug classes cause or exacerbate breast pain by raising prolactin (antipsychotics, metoclopramide), altering hormonal milieu (oral contraceptives, HRT, tamoxifen, spironolactone), or through mechanisms not yet fully characterised (SSRIs, digoxin, methyldopa), together affecting an estimated 10-15% of mastodynia cases.

Subareolar ductal dilatation and periductal inflammation cause a burning or drawing pain behind the nipple and areola; the inflammation may be subclinical or produce frank periareolar redness and discharge, and it tends to be recurrent and non-cyclical.

Costochondritis, intercostal muscle strain, thoracic facet joint dysfunction, and pectoralis major injury produce anterior chest pain that is perceived as breast pain; the key diagnostic feature is reproduction of the pain by pressing directly on the costal cartilage or chest wall rather than the breast parenchyma.

High dietary fat intake has been associated with cyclical breast pain in observational studies, possibly by altering the fatty acid composition of breast cell membranes and increasing their hormonal sensitivity; caffeine has a weaker evidence base but is widely reported by patients to worsen symptoms.

riskFactors.factor: Reproductive age and active menstrual cycling
riskFactors.description: Cyclical breast pain is almost exclusively a disorder of menstruating women, driven by the hormonal swings of ovulation and the luteal phase; it typically resolves at menopause unless hormone therapy is used.
Oral contraceptives, combined hormone replacement therapy, and fertility drugs can induce or worsen breast pain; adjusting the formulation, route, or dose often reduces symptoms.
Diets high in saturated fat and low in fibre may alter the circulating oestrogen profile and increase fatty acid incorporation into breast tissue cell membranes, heightening hormonal sensitivity.

Heavier breast tissue exerts greater mechanical tension on Cooper ligaments and skin, and an ill-fitting bra concentrates pressure unevenly; both contribute to non-cyclical musculoskeletal breast pain.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of benign breast disease
riskFactors.description: Fibrocystic change and breast density, which are partly heritable, are associated with a higher frequency of both cyclical and non-cyclical breast pain.

Caffeine and other methylxanthines may influence breast tissue through phosphodiesterase inhibition, and patient-reported associations between coffee intake and cyclical breast pain are common, though controlled trial evidence is modest.

Higher levels of anxiety and depression independently predict worse pain scores and poorer response to treatment in women with mastodynia; stress amplifies central pain sensitisation and lowers pain thresholds.
Smoking is linked to periductal mastitis and duct ectasia, which cause a specific pattern of non-cyclical subareolar breast pain; smoking cessation is an important part of managing these conditions.

affectedDemographics: Menstruating women aged 30-50, in whom cyclical mastodynia predominates
Perimenopausal women whose cycles are becoming irregular and oestrogen levels fluctuate widely
Women using combined oral contraceptives or hormone replacement therapy
Postmenopausal women with non-cyclical pain, often linked to benign structural changes
Breastfeeding and recently postpartum women, in whom mastitis and duct ectasia are important causes
Women with dense or fibrocystic breast tissue as identified on mammography
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of mastodynia begins with a thorough history that characterises the pain pattern — cyclical versus non-cyclical, bilateral versus unilateral, diffuse versus focal — along with a review of medications, menstrual history, and any accompanying nipple discharge or skin changes. A carefully performed clinical breast examination is essential: palpation of all four quadrants in both sitting and supine positions, examination of the axillae, and specific pressing of the costal cartilages and pectoralis insertion to identify a chest-wall component. The Cardiff Breast Pain Chart, in which a woman maps her pain daily across at least two cycles, is a simple validated tool that reliably distinguishes cyclical from non-cyclical patterns and guides treatment decisions. In women under 35 who have a normal examination, no imaging is required for typical cyclical pain; in women over 35, or when a focal abnormality is palpated, mammography and ultrasound are performed to exclude structural pathology. Ultrasound alone is preferred in women under 35 to avoid radiation exposure. Breast MRI is reserved for high-risk surveillance, equivocal findings, or pre-surgical planning and is not part of routine mastodynia workup. Needle sampling — fine-needle aspiration or core biopsy — is done when imaging identifies a suspicious solid lesion. Blood tests (oestrogen, progesterone, prolactin, TSH) are ordered when hormonal dysfunction is clinically suspected but are not part of routine assessment for typical cyclical pain. The critical differential to address at every visit is primary breast cancer, which causes pain as a sole presenting symptom in under 3% of cases but must not be missed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: To identify localised tenderness, a discrete lump, skin change, or chest-wall reproduction that directs further investigation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician examines both breasts with you sitting and lying down, pressing gently on the breast tissue and the underlying rib junctions; it takes about five minutes and is not painful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A daily diary completed over two menstrual cycles to classify pain as cyclical or non-cyclical, document severity, and track treatment response.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Each day you mark the presence and severity of breast pain on a calendar chart; results are reviewed at the next appointment to guide management decisions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects calcifications, masses, or architectural distortion that could underlie focal pain, particularly in women over 35 or postmenopausal women.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Each breast is briefly compressed between two plates while an X-ray image is taken; the procedure takes about 15 minutes and may cause temporary pressure discomfort.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises cysts, solid masses, duct dilatation, and inflammation; preferred over mammography in women under 35 because it avoids ionising radiation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A handheld probe coated in gel is moved over the breast surface; it is painless and usually takes 10-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes hyperprolactinaemia (from a pituitary microadenoma or medication) and hypothyroidism as hormonal contributors to breast pain.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A routine blood test from a vein in the arm; a slightly elevated prolactin needs repeating fasting, as a single stress-elevated result is common.

diagnosticTests.test: Core needle biopsy or fine-needle aspiration cytology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides tissue or cellular diagnosis of a focal abnormality detected on imaging, distinguishing benign from malignant lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under local anaesthetic and ultrasound guidance, a hollow needle removes a small tissue sample; the area is sore for a day or two afterwards.

treatmentOverview: Most women with cyclical mastodynia are effectively managed with reassurance and simple self-care measures, and spontaneous resolution occurs in about 30% within three months without treatment. The foundation is confirming that the pain is benign, explaining the hormonal mechanism, and addressing modifiable factors such as a poorly fitting bra, high caffeine intake, and relevant medications. When symptoms are moderate to severe and interfere with daily life, first-line pharmacological options in the UK and internationally include topical NSAIDs applied directly to the painful breast — which achieve therapeutic tissue concentrations while minimising systemic absorption — and topical diclofenac has the strongest evidence base among topical agents. Evening primrose oil (gamolenic acid 240-480 mg daily) is widely used as a first-line option in the UK based on older trials, though more recent Cochrane analysis is less conclusive. For women who have failed first-line measures, hormonal treatments — notably danazol, tamoxifen, and bromocriptine — reduce cyclical pain in the majority of patients but carry significant side-effect profiles that limit long-term use. Surgery has no role in mastodynia itself but is sometimes performed for underlying pathology such as a complex cyst, fibroadenoma, or duct ectasia that is driving persistent non-cyclical pain. Referral to a specialist breast clinic is warranted for women who do not respond to two or three months of first-line treatment, in whom imaging is abnormal, or in whom the pain causes significant psychological distress or impairment of daily activities.

medicalTreatments.name: Topical diclofenac gel
medicalTreatments.description: An NSAID applied directly to the affected breast area twice daily; penetrates breast tissue sufficiently to reduce local prostaglandin-driven pain while limiting systemic side effects. First-line recommendation from the Association of Breast Surgery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces pain scores by approximately 50% in controlled studies; responder rates around 60-70% for both cyclical and non-cyclical breast pain.

medicalTreatments.name: Evening primrose oil (gamolenic acid)
medicalTreatments.description: Contains linolenic acid, which is thought to normalise the saturated-to-unsaturated fatty acid ratio in breast tissue and reduce hormonal sensitivity. Typical dose 240-480 mg daily taken for at least 3 months. Widely used in the UK as first-line.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates around 45-58% in cyclical breast pain in older randomised trials; more recent systematic review evidence is equivocal.

medicalTreatments.name: Tamoxifen (10-20 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: A selective oestrogen receptor modulator that blocks oestrogen action in breast tissue; highly effective for cyclical and non-cyclical mastodynia but limited by side effects including hot flushes, irregular periods, and rare thromboembolic events. Typically used for 3-6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces cyclical breast pain in approximately 70-80% of women in randomised trials; one of the most effective pharmacological options.

medicalTreatments.name: Danazol (100-200 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: An androgen that suppresses gonadotrophin secretion and reduces oestrogen-driven breast stimulation; the only drug licensed specifically for mastodynia in some countries. Side effects include acne, weight gain, voice changes, and hepatotoxicity at higher doses.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective in about 70% of cyclical and 31% of non-cyclical cases; currently used as second-line due to its side-effect profile.

medicalTreatments.name: Bromocriptine (2.5-5 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: A dopamine agonist that lowers prolactin levels and reduces breast tissue sensitivity; used for cyclical pain with elevated or high-normal prolactin. Side effects of nausea, dizziness, and hypotension limit adherence.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates around 47-54% in cyclical mastodynia; generally considered less favourable than tamoxifen due to tolerability.

medicalTreatments.name: Adjustment or cessation of causative medications
medicalTreatments.description: When breast pain follows initiation of an SSRI, antipsychotic, combined hormonal contraceptive, or HRT, dose reduction, formulation change (e.g. switching to lower-oestrogen pill or transdermal HRT), or substitution with a non-offending agent often resolves symptoms without additional pharmacotherapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Complete resolution of medication-induced mastodynia occurs in approximately 60-80% of cases after appropriate medication adjustment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Supportive bra fitting
alternativeTreatments.description: A professionally fitted, supportive bra worn day and night reduces mechanical loading on Cooper ligaments and skin, and is the most consistently reported non-pharmacological intervention to reduce breast pain intensity.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary fat reduction and flaxseed supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: A low-fat, high-fibre diet and supplementation with ground flaxseed (25 g daily) have been associated with reduced cyclical breast pain in small controlled studies, possibly by lowering circulating oestrogen levels through altered enterohepatic cycling.

alternativeTreatments.name: Relaxation and stress management techniques
alternativeTreatments.description: Mindfulness, cognitive behavioural therapy, and relaxation training have shown benefit in randomised trials for chronic breast pain, partly by reducing central sensitisation and catastrophisation that amplify pain perception.

whySeeSpecialist: A breast surgeon or specialist breast clinic should be consulted when pain is accompanied by a new lump, skin change, or nipple discharge; when it is non-cyclical, focal, and persistent beyond three months; when imaging has been performed and shows an abnormality requiring tissue sampling; when first-line treatment has failed after two cycles; or when the pain causes significant psychological distress or disruption to daily life. A specialist can exclude structural causes, provide tailored hormonal treatment, and — importantly — offer the definitive reassurance that the underlying breast tissue is normal, which has been shown in trials to reduce pain scores independently of any pharmacological intervention.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a breast surgeon or consultant with dedicated breast clinic sessions, access to on-site mammography and ultrasound, and experience with the Cardiff breast pain scoring system for classifying and monitoring mastodynia. For cases driven by hormonal medication, a gynaecologist or endocrinologist with an interest in women's health may be a better fit. Ask whether the clinic follows Association of Breast Surgery or national breast guidelines, whether specialist breast nurses provide follow-up, and what the typical waiting time for urgent imaging is.
preventionStrategies: Wear a well-fitted, supportive bra throughout the day, and consider a soft sports-style bra at night during symptomatic phases.
Limit caffeine and high-fat food intake, particularly in the 10 days before menstruation if you experience cyclical breast pain.
If you start a new medication known to cause breast pain, record the onset and discuss formulation alternatives with the prescribing clinician early.
Maintain a healthy body weight, as higher BMI is associated with more severe cyclical and non-cyclical breast symptoms.
If you smoke, seek cessation support — smoking is directly linked to periductal mastitis, a cause of recurrent focal breast pain.
Keep a monthly pain diary for at least two cycles before seeking care; pattern recognition guides both self-management and clinical treatment decisions.
lifestyleModifications: Reduce or eliminate caffeine-containing drinks during the luteal phase of the cycle as an initial low-risk step.
Adopt a low-saturated-fat, high-fibre diet and consider 25 g ground flaxseed daily as part of a broader dietary change.
Choose a bra with wide straps and firm support rather than underwired styles during flares, which concentrate pressure differently.
Use heat application (a warm pad or hot water bottle wrapped in a cloth) for 15-20 minutes to relax breast and chest-wall musculature during acute pain episodes.
Practise stress reduction techniques — yoga, mindfulness, or regular aerobic exercise — which lower cortisol and reduce pain amplification.
Keep a pain and menstrual diary to identify triggers and track whether first-line measures are working.

recommended: Low-fat diet with reduced saturated and trans fats, which may lower circulating oestrogen and reduce breast tissue sensitivity
High-fibre foods including whole grains, legumes, and vegetables to support oestrogen metabolism via the gut
Ground flaxseed (up to 25 g daily) as a source of lignans that modulate oestrogen receptor activity
Omega-3-rich foods such as oily fish, walnuts, and chia seeds to reduce the ratio of saturated to essential fatty acids in breast tissue membranes
Adequate vitamin E-rich foods (sunflower seeds, almonds, avocado) as antioxidants that some small trials associate with reduced cyclical pain
avoid: High caffeine intake from coffee, strong tea, cola, and chocolate — many women report symptom improvement with reduction
High saturated fat from red meat, full-fat dairy, and processed foods
Excess added sugar and refined carbohydrates, which drive insulin surges and may amplify hormonal fluctuations
Alcohol in excess, which impairs hepatic oestrogen metabolism

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise — at least 150 minutes per week of walking, cycling, or swimming — supports healthy hormonal cycling and reduces the central sensitisation that amplifies breast pain. A supportive sports bra during exercise is essential to minimise mechanical breast movement. There is no need to restrict exercise because of breast pain; on the contrary, physical activity is associated with lower levels of breast pain severity in prospective studies. During acute pain episodes, gentle movement is preferable to rest.
dailyManagement: Complete a daily pain diary (Cardiff Chart or equivalent) to track patterns, identify triggers, and demonstrate progress to clinicians.
Apply topical diclofenac gel to the painful area after showering, twice daily as directed, and allow it to dry before dressing.
Take evening primrose oil capsules consistently with food; the full effect takes up to three months of regular use.
Wear a supportive bra throughout the day and consider leaving a soft bra on overnight during peak pain phases.
Time any NSAID tablets (e.g. ibuprofen) to take 30-60 minutes before anticipated peak pain, such as in the late luteal phase.
Return for review if pain has not improved after two to three months of treatment — the plan may need to be escalated.
prognosis: Cyclical mastodynia has a good natural history: about 30% of women experience spontaneous resolution within three months, and a further 20-30% are adequately controlled with conservative measures such as a supportive bra, caffeine reduction, and evening primrose oil. Of women treated with effective pharmacological agents such as tamoxifen, around 70-80% achieve meaningful pain reduction. However, about 60% of those who respond will have a relapse within two years of stopping treatment, indicating that the hormonal susceptibility of the breast tissue persists throughout the reproductive years. Non-cyclical pain resolves less readily — spontaneous remission occurs in about 50% over two years — and the outlook depends heavily on identifying and addressing the underlying structural or medication-related cause. Mastodynia does not increase the risk of breast cancer, and this reassurance itself is one of the most powerful therapeutic interventions, with studies demonstrating measurable pain reduction in women who receive detailed explanation alone.
recoveryTimeline: Women starting topical diclofenac or evening primrose oil should allow at least 8-12 weeks before assessing response, since breast tissue composition changes slowly. In the first two to four weeks, reduction in peak pain severity is the realistic target. With tamoxifen, meaningful benefit is usually apparent within 4-6 weeks. For medication-induced breast pain, symptoms typically improve within two to four weeks of dose reduction or switching. Periductal mastitis and duct ectasia-driven pain are more variable, improving over weeks to months depending on whether antibiotics (for active inflammation) or surgical duct excision is required.
complications: Anxiety and depression secondary to chronic breast pain — affect up to 30% of women with persistent mastodynia and require psychological as well as physical management.
Disrupted sleep, reduced sexual activity, and limitation of daily tasks — severe mastodynia scores on quality-of-life tools are comparable to those of other chronic pain conditions.

Over-investigation and diagnostic delays when the clinician fails to use structured assessment (Cardiff Chart, clinical examination), sometimes leading to unnecessary imaging, biopsy, or referral cycles.

Side effects of second-line pharmacological therapy — danazol and tamoxifen carry risks of thromboembolic events, liver enzyme elevation, bone density loss, and hormonal side effects that require monitoring.

Missed underlying pathology — focally severe or treatment-resistant non-cyclical pain can very occasionally mask an inflammatory carcinoma, abscess, or other structural lesion that requires its own management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mastitis without abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mastitis causes acute unilateral breast pain with visible redness, warmth, and systemic fever, whereas mastodynia is typically bilateral, cyclical, and without signs of infection.

confusedWithConditions.name: Breast cancer
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Breast cancer rarely presents with pain alone — a firm, irregular, fixed lump, skin dimpling, or nipple inversion are the distinguishing features requiring urgent assessment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Premenstrual tension syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: PMS includes breast tenderness as one of many symptoms — mood changes, bloating, headache — whereas cyclical mastodynia describes breast pain as the predominant complaint.

relatedConditions.name: Breast cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Ruling out breast cancer is the central reason women with mastodynia seek care; cancer rarely causes isolated pain but must always be excluded.

relatedConditions.name: Mastitis without abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: Mastitis is a direct differential of acute unilateral breast pain and shares the clinical setting of the breast clinic.

relatedConditions.name: Premenstrual tension syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Breast pain is the most common physical symptom of PMS, and the two diagnoses frequently coexist in the same patient.

relatedConditions.name: Nipple discharge
relatedConditions.relevance: Nipple discharge accompanies periductal mastitis and duct ectasia, both recognised causes of non-cyclical breast pain.

faqs.question: What is mastodynia?
faqs.answer: Mastodynia is the medical term for breast pain. It affects about 70% of women at some point and is most commonly cyclical, tied to the menstrual cycle. Most cases are benign and do not indicate cancer.

faqs.question: Is breast pain a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Breast cancer causes pain as an isolated symptom in fewer than 3% of cases. Pain accompanied by a new lump, skin change, or nipple discharge needs prompt assessment, but breast pain alone — especially cyclical — is almost always benign.

faqs.question: What causes cyclical breast pain?
faqs.answer: Rising oestrogen and progesterone in the second half of the menstrual cycle increase breast tissue water retention and hormonal sensitivity, producing the aching heaviness typical of cyclical mastodynia. It usually eases within one to two days of the period starting.

faqs.question: How is breast pain diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a clinical breast examination and a pain diary completed over two menstrual cycles. Women over 35 or those with a focal abnormality are referred for mammography and ultrasound. Blood tests for prolactin and thyroid function are ordered if a hormonal cause is suspected.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for cyclical breast pain?
faqs.answer: First-line options are a well-fitted supportive bra, caffeine reduction, and topical diclofenac gel or evening primrose oil. If these fail after 8-12 weeks, a doctor may prescribe tamoxifen or danazol, which are effective in around 70% of cases.

faqs.question: Does caffeine make breast pain worse?
faqs.answer: Many women report that reducing caffeine from coffee, tea, cola, and chocolate improves cyclical breast pain, though controlled trial evidence is modest. A two-month trial of caffeine reduction is a low-risk first step that costs nothing.

faqs.question: Can a bra affect breast pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. A professionally fitted, supportive bra reduces mechanical loading on ligaments and skin that contributes to non-cyclical breast pain. Wearing a soft supportive bra at night during symptomatic phases is also commonly recommended.

faqs.question: Will breast pain go away on its own?
faqs.answer: About 30% of women with cyclical mastodynia experience spontaneous resolution within three months. Non-cyclical pain is less likely to resolve without addressing its cause. Most cases are manageable with self-care measures if a structural cause has been excluded.

faqs.question: Is evening primrose oil effective for breast pain?
faqs.answer: Evening primrose oil (gamolenic acid 240-480 mg daily) shows response rates of 45-58% in cyclical breast pain in older controlled trials. It needs to be taken consistently for at least three months. Newer systematic reviews are more cautious about its evidence base.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor for breast pain?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if pain is accompanied by a new lump, skin change, or nipple discharge; if it is non-cyclical and persists beyond three months; if it is unilateral and postmenopausal; or if it is severe enough to disrupt sleep or daily activities.

faqs.question: Can the contraceptive pill cause breast pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Combined oral contraceptives can trigger or worsen breast pain, particularly in the first few months of use. Switching to a lower-oestrogen formulation or a progestogen-only pill often resolves the problem. Discuss alternatives with your prescribing clinician.

faqs.question: What is non-cyclical breast pain?
faqs.answer: Non-cyclical breast pain is unrelated to the menstrual cycle. It tends to be focal, constant, or burning, and may arise from benign breast disease, duct ectasia, medication side effects, or a chest-wall disorder. It requires imaging if persistent.

faqs.question: Can chest-wall problems feel like breast pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Costochondritis (costochondral junction inflammation) and intercostal muscle strain refer pain to the breast area. The distinguishing feature is that pressing on the rib cartilage reproduces the pain, whereas pressing on the breast itself does not.

faqs.question: Does mastodynia affect breastfeeding?
faqs.answer: Breast pain during lactation is usually due to mastitis, engorgement, or poor latch rather than mastodynia per se. Mastitis causes redness, warmth, and fever requiring antibiotics. Persistent postpartum breast pain without these features may be investigated as in non-pregnant women.

faqs.question: What dietary changes help breast pain?
faqs.answer: A low-fat, high-fibre diet and 25 g daily of ground flaxseed have been associated with reduced cyclical breast pain in small controlled studies. Reducing saturated fat and cutting caffeine are the two most evidence-supported dietary measures.

faqs.question: Is breast pain related to anxiety?
faqs.answer: Higher levels of anxiety and psychological stress independently predict worse breast pain scores. Stress amplifies pain sensitivity throughout the body. Addressing anxiety through counselling, relaxation, or mindfulness can reduce breast pain severity alongside other management.

faqs.question: Can tamoxifen treat breast pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Tamoxifen at 10-20 mg daily reduces cyclical breast pain in approximately 70-80% of women in randomised trials, making it one of the most effective pharmacological options. It is typically used for 3-6 months and requires monitoring for side effects.

faqs.question: What investigations are done for breast pain?
faqs.answer: A clinical examination and pain diary are the first steps. Women over 35 or those with a focal finding have mammography and ultrasound. Blood tests for prolactin and thyroid function are added when a hormonal cause is suspected. Most investigations are done to exclude cancer rather than to diagnose mastodynia itself.

faqs.question: Does breast pain mean I have a cyst?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Breast cysts are more likely to cause a palpable lump than pain, though a large tense cyst can cause local tenderness. Ultrasound will diagnose a cyst definitively, and aspiration of a symptomatic cyst can provide rapid pain relief.

faqs.question: Is mastodynia more common on one side?
faqs.answer: Cyclical mastodynia is usually bilateral and diffuse, predominantly in the upper outer quadrants. Non-cyclical pain is more often unilateral and focal. Persistent unilateral pain, especially in a postmenopausal woman, warrants imaging even if examination is normal.

metaDescription: Mastodynia (breast pain) affects 70% of women. Learn cyclical vs non-cyclical causes, diagnosis, and evidence-based treatments. Doctor-reviewed.
